sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

I chaired my final committee meeting last night.  Over the years I reckon that I’ve attended about 130 such meetings and chaired about 115.  We used to meet immediately before club nights but that was all a bit rushed and, when meetings inevitably ran over, new members and guest speakers got ignored.  Meetings used to be held every month but, when that became too onerous, we changed to holding them every other month.  When I first joined the committee, drinks but not travel, were charged to expenses.  We held committee meetings on Zoom during lockdown.  But enough reminiscing: what matters of critical importance did we discuss last night?

The numbers attending the FlyIn were lower than we’d hoped, mostly due to the weather forecast, but at least it didn’t rain.  Bizarrely, the fewer the participants, the more money we make: people pay upfront but if they don’t turn up we don’t have to pay the campsite owner. We could probably promote the event better for next year; and establishing a single Instant Messaging group at an early stage would probably help communication.  Provisional figures suggest that we made a profit of about £500.  Added to previous profits we now have about £1500 ‘ring fenced’ for the FlyIn.  At some stage the committee will have to decide whether to return some of these funds to our central account, but we agreed that, whilst we continue to try to grow the event, that decision will be put on hold for another year.

Invitations to the Farmers’ Dinner have been circulated to farmers and members alike with a deadline of 8 Oct.

The main aim of the Sep committee meeting is to assess our current financial position and forecast our planned expenditure for next year.  Armed with this information we can recommend subscription levels, and the contribution to the Flying Fund, to the membership at the AGM in December.  I won’t go into the detailed figures here, but our recommendation (spoiler alert) will be to maintain both at their current levels.

As reported last month we have a cunning plan to fill most of the committee roles next year.  Alas, Ed Cleasby has decided to step down as competitions officer: he feels that it doesn’t justify a post on the committee, and that he can achieve what he wants outside the committee.  Until such time as the club decides to get involved in team competitions again, it’s true that the job isn’t onerous, but we will need to find somebody to enter the club into the XC League and co-ordinate the allocation of XC mugs.  Whether or not you’re enthusiastic for the club to enter team competitions (like the BCC), but feel able to stand as our competitions officer, then please get in touch.

sections.title: DHPC AGM Notification
sections.content:
Thursday December 4th 2025. 7.30 for 8 pm start
Horse and Farrier, Bridge St, Otley. LS21 1BQ

All members of the Dales Hang Gliding and Paragliding Club are invited to attend the AGM, which will be followed by the presentation of the coveted club trophies and mugs for the 2025 season.

All proposals will be published in the November edition of Skywords and on the club forum. The Treasurer's report and reports from the other current committee members will be published on the forum by November 19th (members only). There will be no opportunity to raise new issues for voting after 1st November, or on the night at the AGM. Voting on all proposals will be at the AGM in person only.

As well as setting membership subscriptions and contributions to the Flying Fund for the coming year, all committee posts are up for re-election, as per the constitution. Members are free to stand for any post - none of the current incumbents will be offended if you wish to have a go at their role. Committee membership simply requires a little spare time and a willingness to put something back into the club. If you want to have a non-committal conversation about a role, then please contact the Chairman, Martin Baxter, at chairman@dhpc.org.uk , or Secretary Tam Morrison at secretary@dhpc.org.uk

sections.title: Urgent Action Needed from All
sections.content:
From the BHPA on the CAA Electronic Conspicuity Consultation

Please Respond by 6th October 2025.

The CAA is consulting on the future of Electronic Conspicuity (EC) — and it could directly affect every hang glider, paraglider, paramotor and powered hang glider pilot in the UK. The CAA's preferred option is mandatory ADS-B.

BHPA’s position is clear:

ADS-B or transponders are impractical and disproportionate for our lightweight, cockpit-less aircraft, and may reduce safety

EC for HG/PG must remain voluntary and limited to simple “broadcast-out” devices.

Full systems should rest with powered aircraft and drones, not us.

This will affect all pilots, BHPA members or not. If we don’t respond, we risk being forced to carry kit that doesn’t work for our sports.

To support and encourage responses from our community, the BHPA has produced a summary of its consultation response, so that all UK pilots — members and non-members alike — can see the BHPA position, read the consultation papers, and submit their own views.

Make your voice heard. Don’t leave it to others. Respond to the CAA by 6th October 2025.

https://consultations.caa.co.uk/safety-and-airspace-regulation-group/ec-conops/

Advice to fill it in…. https://www.bhpa.co.uk/pdf/news/CAA_CAP_3140_EC_consultation.pdf 

Thanks.

sections.title: Farmers’ Dinner
sections.content: The Knights Table

subsections.title: Saturday 18th October 2025
subsections.content:
7:30pm for 8pm dinner     

We are very lucky that we don’t pay any site fees in the Dales.  Instead we invite the farmers and their partners to a slap up meal every year. Over the years we have found this to be very cost effective: a little goodwill goes a very long way.  The committee does most of the hosting, and this year we will also be offering free tickets to the first club coaches who respond, by way of thanks for giving up their spare time.  Other members are most welcome, but I’m afraid that you’ll have to pay.  £27 for 3 courses isn’t bad these days, and getting to know the farmers can be very handy when you need a lift back up the hill…

As with the previous couple of years, we’re returning to the excellent food and service of Knights Table at Stainforth.  Please aim to meet in the bar for drinks from 7.30pm.  The dress code is Smart Casual, but please wear whatever makes you feel comfortable.

subsections.title: Booking
subsections.content:
If you would like to attend, please respond to Dave Bradwell, treasurer@dhpc.org.uk  by 8 October 2025.  He’ll need to know your full name (first & surname) and then the three numbers of your preferred dishes.  If you’re bringing a guest, then he’ll need the same for them.  If you need to pay he’ll tell you how to do that.

We look forward to seeing you there.

subsections.title: Menu - £27 for 3 courses
subsections.content:
Starters

1 Gyozas - crispy roasted pork dumplings deep fried served with hoi sin dipping sauce and pickled cucumbers

2 Samosas - spiced potato and spinach samosas with bowl of tarka dal and mango chutney - V, VE

3 Soup of the Day - a piping hot bowl of our homemade soup served with homemade bread and butter - GF

4 Popcorn Prawns - plump Greenland prawns tossed in salt and pepper coating deep fried  crispy and served with pea shoots and sweet chilli dipping sauce - GF

Main Courses

5 Steak and Onion Pie - slow cooked Maudsley farm beef with rich local ale gravy in an all-butter shortcrust pastry, served with vegetable selection and chips

6 Fish and Chips - fresh Fleetwood haddock fillet deep fried in a light ale batter served with  mushy peas and chips - GF

7 Grandma’s Cheese Pie - an old family recipe, three cheeses, onion and potato oven baked in a shortcrust  pastry pie served with salad and chips - V

8 Sheep and Pigs - slow roasted crispy belly pork, Maudsley farm lamb chop over smoked ham bubble n  squeak in a red wine and peppercorn gravy with a side of seasonal veg - GF

9 Goan Vegetable Curry - cauliflower, red pepper, green beans and potato cooked in our mild Goan spice paste with tomato, coconut and tamarind served with vegetable pilaf and bhel puri - GF, V, VE

Desserts

10 Blackberry & Apple Crumble Tart with vanilla ice cream

11 White Chocolate Cheesecake with blueberry compote - GF

12 Chocolate and Stem Ginger Meringues layered with whipped cream and chocolate sauce - GF

13 White Chocolate and Raspberry Tiramisu

Vegan desserts available on request

GF – Gluten Free Available V – Vegetarian VE – Vegan Available Please inform a member of staff if you have any allergies, intolerances or special dietary requirements. While we try our best to  accommodate allergies, we are not an allergen-free kitchen, so we cannot guarantee everything is 100% allergen free.

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

A final call for folks who are in a position to do their pilot rating exam, let me know at coaching@dhpc.org.uk to be included in revision sessions and exam planning.

subsections.title: “Fly like a bird – think like a pilot”
subsections.content:
- is the catchphrase for a campaign in New Zealand encouraging free flight pilots to adopt a more safety-conscious approach. As the nights draw in I thought I’d pass on this fantastic resource for improving your flight safety whilst on the ground.

The New Zealand Hang Gliding and Paragliding Association has been working on issues around safety for several years, and produced an award-winning report two years ago.

That report has formed the basis for a new national strategy when it comes to continued pilot development. Built around three pillars it includes knowing your environment, knowing your plan and knowing yourself.

“It’s easy to forget we’re not birds and don’t have their instincts – or their wings,” the NZHGPA say. “This is not our natural environment. We have to think more like pilots and be more deliberate with our intentions and our actions.”

They add: “The overriding determinant of pilot safety in hang gliding and paragliding is the quality of pilot decision-making … [it’s important to] focus on the thinking part of your flying as well as the doing part.”

More details on the findings and advice for pilots can be found on their website at nzhgpa.org.nz/thinklikeapilot.

sections.title: Cow Close Fell
sections.content:
Stephen Craven reports

Steven Lund, the farmer at Cow Close Fell, has complained that he counted 17 pilots in the air at one time in March/Aprill.  It’s a particular problem at this time of year when frightening ewes can lead to an increase in still births. The  site guide limits us to 6: please respect the rule.  We’ve had this problem before and nearly lost the site.

South Sites Officer.

sections.title: Nont Sarahs
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Following yet another near miss between a paraglider and a model glider at Nont Sarahs the relationship between us and the modellers has reached a new low. Whilst the PSC attempts to negotiate some clear deconfliction measures, please be aware that the situation is sensitive and emotions are running high. If you do fly there try to maintain a good safety margin and if there is an incident, keep your cool and try not to inflame the situation.

sections.title: Safety
sections.content:
Clive Sury reports

Weight Watchers

 

I like to think that I’m reasonably fit given that I play racket sports regularly, undertake brisk walks up and down hills by Ilkley Moor most days and not least, walk up bigger hills in the Dales with my paragliding kit.  Despite this, some of you will have heard me say that ‘I’m a one hill guy!’ with only enough energy to climb up once to take off.  And even then, with me being a lot slower than others and somewhat tired once at the launch site.

I’ve always been conscious of this and I’m aware that as I get older there’s a risk that this not only detracts from enjoying the overall experience of flying in the Dales, but it also presents a risk.  As the motorway overhead gantry states – ‘tiredness kills’.  OK, it may not be as serious as that, but it does mean that I must take time to recuperate before taking off, as well as miss out on flying again on the same day.

Therefore, following the mantra that if you want a different result do something differently, I’ve taken the plunge on changing to a lightweight harness. More specifically, getting an Advance Weightless harness & backpack and an Advance SQR Light 2 Reserve.  This has taken a massive 4 kilos off my previous kit!  Plus, I’m looking to shave off another 400g through changing varios from an Air3 to a Naviter OMNI.  Further to this, as a tweak I’m now using a lightweight concertina bag.  A detailed breakdown can be seen below:

Advance weightless harness

2.0 kg

Advance SQR Light 2 reserve

0.9 kg

Skywalk Arak 2 105

4.6 kg

Supair glider bag

0.2 kg

Advance ULS 70l backpack

0.5 kg

Subtotal: wing; harness; reserv kit

8.2 kg

Helmet; gloves; phone; vario; radio equipment

1.7 kg

Water 1 litres including camel back

1.2 kg

Other: paper; keys; glasses; walking poles

0.9 kg

Total

12 kg

 

Further to this I also focused on losing weight.  I’ve lost 3.6 kg since the summer and am working on losing another kilo, possibly two.  I don’t want to fall into the trap here of being a dieting bore but suffice to say that the best weight loss diet is the one that works for you.

The positive outcome to this is that walking up Gragareth and Windbank with the revised kit was a lot more comfortable.  I wasn’t as tired, could get ready to fly more quickly and even went up Windbank for a second flight last Sunday.  Happy days.

However, there are safety considerations in doing this.  The first obvious one is that where previously I was above the recommended mid weight for my wing, I’m now on the lighter end of the parameters.  At 90kg total weight, I’m above the recommended minimum weight of 85kg but now need to be away from potential flying characteristics from being light on the wing – that is, potential for more collapses.  So far, the Arak 2 has been a very stable wing for me and I’m happy to stay with it for now.  Also, if I go to a site without a climb up, I may take more water to act as additional ballast.  Plus, in the winter wearing heavier clothes and boots will add marginal additional weight.

Another area of concern is the flying dynamics of a lightweight harness.  I was advised that it would be more ‘active’ and was afforded a trial before committing to buy it.  My test flight was in turbulent punchy air so it was difficult for me to ascertain if it was the harness or air that was lively.  However, compared to the conditions I’ve flown in, I didn’t find it overly uncomfortable.  Plus, without meaning to sound contrary, I did find turning in it much more dynamic and responsive.  Frankly a joy to turn in.

Lastly regarding safety, a key element is that the padding is not nearly as much in the Weightless versus the Supair Delight 4 which has a seat board and thick padded cushion underneath.  The Advance Weightless does have foam padding in the seat which can be added to with the backpack rolled up (or other item such as the glider bag), as well as back padding.  This is a consideration with mitigating action here for me being to keep my mid-B stable wing and safety awareness when flying and landing.  Plus, go keep working on my ground handling skills.

Having spoken to a few other pilots about this, an obvious question of how much this cost has been asked.  I think it would be disrespectful to Steve at the Sick and Wrong to directly answer this.  What I will say though is that buying second hand and trading in old kit has made it affordable and for me, value for money.  Also, buying through Steve has allowed me to try it out before committing and have the harness including the new reserve fitted in the shop

Finally, I remember reading Bruce Goldsmith stating that as a rule of thumb, having a fairing on a harness adds approximately 0.5 to the glide ratio.  Who says you can’t buy performance?  Given my flying skills, any advantage is welcome haha.  In any event, so far, I’ve enjoyed the change and look forward to climbing more hills, more often.

 

Safe flying,

Clive.

 

P.S.  Re the photo. No, I haven’t been enlisted as a test pilot for Advance, but it was too good an opportunity to miss to share a picture of my doppelganger in this harness. If only I had their flying ability too!

Crossing borders

Recently I’ve flown at Pendle, Lancashire.  Yes I know, there be dragons.  However, counter to these fears I can say that my experience was very welcoming.  On arriving at the site Brian Stewart and others made us feel very welcome, sharing their knowledge of the site and what the conditions were like.  I’m currently attempting Ed Cleasby’s Northern Challenge Series tasks which mean that I now often land out from attempting a waypoint. The consequence of this was that whilst I made it back to the lower ridge I still had a walk up the main hill at one end.  During this Brian and 2 others enquired about my welfare on the radio.  As it was, I didn’t need help, however, crucially I may have done and therefore, for 3 separate people to care to ask is impressive.  Thank you and the Pennine Club safety culture here is commendable.

Separately I went to Model Ridge for the first time this week. I was fortunate in being introduced to Nick Kavanagh who’s the NYSC Senior Club Coach.  Nick provided a comprehensive overview of the site, including its hazards, landing options and likely areas for lift.  Further to this, he gave me some invaluable coaching on the day and even came down the hill and helped me back up after I’d had the ‘initiation’ of a slope landing down it.  And yes, I can vouch that it’s steeper than it looks. All of this was much appreciated.  As was the warm welcome Andrew Nightingale and I received from the other members.

Recognising that many people are members of multiple clubs including the Dales Club, please extend my thanks to everyone for looking after us and for the purposes of this section, their demonstration of safety consciousness for others.

sections.title: First Aid - New Date: 6 December!
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann

Our instructor had to cancel the original date (just as I thought I had the seats filled), so the course has been moved by one week to.

  • 6 December  09:00 - 17:00
  • at the Bradley Village Hall  (map) *
  • Cost ~£65  (less if more than 10 participants)
  • ‘Emergency Outdoor First Aid’ certificate

*  unless we end up sharing with Pennines in which case we may look for more central.

As of 3/10 there are 3 places left, but I am also in contact with Pennines club to fill them. We need a minimum of 10 participants to make this viable, so please do not delay showing your interest or the course may not go ahead this year! Financial commitment will only be required to confirm your seat.

To sign up, contact me on WA or TG or email firstaid@dhpc.org.uk with your name, email and mobile nr.

sections.title: X-Dales & FlyIn
sections.content:
Paul Rowntree reports

The X-Dales had more than head winds in 2025. X-Scotia re-arranged its date to the same weekend (to be cancelled due to weather), the date clashed with an SRS event, competitors now need to be pilot rated and the forecast didn't look too friendly. C'est la vie.

 

Frustratingly for organisers Ed and Rob, they had to abandon their plans for the event but came up with a great alternative - Ribblehead to Langcliffe/Dales FlyIn campsite via a number of turnpoints ..... or do it in the opposite direction. As the crow flies, just 20.1km, with some flying, sold as an easy course that won't take long.  No set start time, start when you think is best on Friday or Saturday in the 9am-1pm window, the quickest wins.  Must finish by 7pm.

 

Hike and Fly competitions are generally won in the air.  There were two flying windows, late Friday, or early Saturday.  Friday was crudely a westerly, Saturday, south easterly.

Six of the club's finest athletes opted for a late Friday start, although their strategies were split. Pete Logan, Chris Kay and Steph Sykes opted to travel upstream.  Ed Cleasby, Mark Pinches and James Maxwell the opposite way.  As forecast, the flying window opened up with those traveling north enjoying a flight from Simon Fell to goal. For those heading south the terrain didn't permit big flights from their position on the course when the wind dropped, but their feet left the ground.  Unfortunately for all of them, the cut-off time arrived before they reached goal.  Possibly good news for everyone, course setter, Ed, described himself as collapsed in a hedge with exhaustion on the side of the road waiting to be picked up - an easy 20km course it wasn’t - next year’s course may include escalators, permission to hitch a lift, catch a train, etc!!

I opted for the Saturday morning start along with a guest to the Dales, Gary Crisp.  We both took the downstream option.  Whilst hiking up Park Fell, I'd established Gary was an ultra marathon runner - someone who runs over 100km non-stop for fun, this wasn't even a training session for Gary.  Whilst establishing that all we needed to do was finish the course in the 10hr window to win, I'd need a miracle flight to have a chance of winning. From the top of Park, we parted ways. I was checking out if an early ridge flight was worth it whilst Gary continued one foot in front of the other. After concluding I'd only manage to fly a km or so, I kept the wing in the bag. I somehow caught up with Gary at the top of Ingleborough, my uphill speed was similar to his, where the wind was already too strong.  After tagging Little Ingleborough, I found a spot to take off on the edge of the bowl going back around Ingleborough towards Simon Fell.  Keeping on the edge of the lift band and venturi effect I was able to creep around the bowl, touch down to walk through a stone wall that was in my way, then fly to the foot of Simon Fell.

I was chuffed to bits - I’d flown c3km saving c4km on foot. However, 10 mins back in hike mode, even trotting on some downhills, Gary appears beside me, he's quick on the ground.  To have a chance of winning, I’d need to fly again whilst Gary was happy to carry his glider for the day.  I hoped there would be somewhere suitable around Moughton Scars but the wind direction wasn't quite right, and was probably too strong anyway. Our differing navigation routes took us separate ways but we met again at roughly the halfway point.  Flying again was unlikely, my only tactic was to try and engage him in a fascinating conversation that he couldn't leave. I managed a minute before he suggested we should make the most of the downhills to run.  I couldn't keep up and he knew it!

Gary finished in 4hrs 16mins.  I stupidly tried to fly again knowing I had nothing to lose in the hope of finding some magical air that carried me back to Langcliffe, but the conditions weren't suitable. That exercise cost me 50 mins.  I finished in 5hrs 22mins covering 26.5km.  

Looking back, I could have flown more into the lift band in the bowl that would have given me more height and possibly try to gain some height on Simon Fell to be able to push on further, but in flying into wind, extra height doesn't convert that well into horizontal distance, flying into the wind is probably a similar speed to Gary running and increasing the risk of being blown back was not on the agenda.

For me, if it wasn't for the Fly-in/X-Dales, I would have looked at the forecast and stayed at home.  As it was, the date was in the diary and I was released from all parenting responsibilities for a couple of days.  Arriving on Friday evening, I flew at Langcliffe Quarry, others completing top to bottoms into the campsite before chewing the cud around the campsite.

The wind curtailed flying plans on Saturday but it was good for ground handling and the inaugural ground handling race. There were various heats and rule adjustments but they couldn't stop Head Coach Pete Logan taking the trophy.  

Other than the strengthening wind taking the marquee for a small flight, it stayed warm and dry and the Saturday night BBQ and campfire were only interrupted by the prize giving of various paragliding kit that was kindly donated by Ozone and others.  Additionally, Ginger Nomad with a stall/van full of kit were on site, there were site tours, human tow launching, walks, etc.

A massive thank you to everyone who helped with the organisation of the weekend.  Many hands aided a quick Sunday morning packing up of the campsite before a full breakfast debrief in Settle.  

(Most photo credits to Pete Logan)

sections.title: Dales Social Nights restart 9th Oct
sections.content:
Stef, Social Sec, reports

And with unbaited glee, the winter socials commence again! 730 for an 8pm start on the Horse and Farrier in Otley.

Our season kicks off on the serious issue of air proxes. Club member Simon leads a discussion entitled:

‘Know thine enemy - an insight into military fast jet flying’

Simon Hulme is a Royal Air Force veteran and fighter pilot who retired in 2022 after a career spanning more than 34 years. His early flying was in the Air Training Corps flying Chipmunks and Venture motor gliders, he completed a flying scholarship at age 17 and joined the RAF aged 18. His operational flying career was on the Tornado GR1/4 initially based in Germany although his flying career has taken him as far afield as Australia and Alaska. After 15 years flying Tornados he ventured into the training world flying Hawk T1s from RAF Valley. After 8 years of staff appointments that finished with a tour as a military diplomat in Ottawa he returned to flying as a Reserve forces officer flying the Hawk T2 at RAF Valley. He has over 5000 hours of flying, the vast majority on fighter aircraft.

sections.title: From the Archive
sections.content:
Next time you’re sweating your way up Whernside spare a thought for these guys in 1995.  It’s a sight that’s unlikely to be repeated.   #Hardcore. #Respect.

Apologies for the quality.  When I initially scanned the images I saved them as pdfs, not realising that Google Docs won’t accept that format. By the time I realised, the hard copy magazines had gone to a better place, so I had to grab screen shots and save them again.  They probably lost a bit of definition in the process.
